ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for Zi Char-style dry crab specifically; closest parent 'crab food product' (FOODON_03302757) used as anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CJW (Crabs) is the closest defensible parent; no specific code for dry-style Zi Char crab preparation exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226955001 (Crab substance) is the closest available anchor; no specific code for Zi Char dry crab preparation.

serving.description: 1 portion (shared dish, approximately 300 g edible portion)
serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving of Asahi Dry Crab at a Zi Char restaurant is approximately 300 g of edible crab meat and shell-on pieces, shared between two to three diners as part of a multi-dish meal.

nutrient_source.title: Crab, cooked, moist heat — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 15139); adapted with dry-style Zi Char seasoning adjustment using IMR Malaysia MyFCD reference for stir-fried crab dishes
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / Institute for Medical Research Malaysia
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Base crab nutrient values derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (cooked crab, NDB 15139 / FDC ID 174202). Sodium and fat values adjusted upward to reflect dry-style Zi Char preparation with butter, oyster sauce, soy sauce, and aromatics, consistent with IMR Malaysia MyFCD stir-fried crab dish references. Energy recalculated using Atwater factors. This is an adapted estimate; exact restaurant formulation is not available in any approved primary source.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of seafood and mixed protein-fat dishes — analogous estimate based on cooked crab and stir-fried seafood dish data; University of Sydney GI Database reference for low-GI seafood preparations
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Zi Char-style dry crab. GI estimated at 30 based on the University of Sydney GI Database classification of cooked crustacean and mixed stir-fried seafood dishes as low-GI foods (GI typically 20–40). The low carbohydrate content (4.2 g per 100 g) and high protein-fat ratio strongly suppress glycemic response. Glycemic load per 300 g serving = (30 × 12.6 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 3.8.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from oyster sauce, soy sauce, and seasoning in dry-style Zi Char preparation
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size of this dish to no more than 150 g per meal, as the estimated sodium content of approximately 520 mg per 100 g (1,560 mg per 300 g serving) represents a substantial proportion of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2,000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~30) and low carbohydrate content (4.2 g per 100 g) with high protein supporting glycemic stability
type2_diabetes.guidance: Asahi Dry Crab has a low estimated glycemic index of approximately 30 and a low glycemic load per serving (~3.8), making it a suitable protein-rich choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-GI accompaniments such as white rice in large quantities.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat from butter and cooking oil, elevated sodium, and dietary cholesterol from crab
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While crab provides beneficial omega-3 fatty acids and lean protein, the dry-style Zi Char preparation adds saturated fat (estimated 2.4 g per 100 g) and high sodium; individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish in moderation and request reduced butter or oil preparation where possible.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Asahi Dry Crab (Zi Char Style) is a Malaysian-Singaporean seafood dish served at Zi Char (煮炒) restaurants, which are casual Chinese-style cook-to-order eateries common across Singapore and Malaysia. The dish features whole or halved crab — typically mud crab or flower crab — cooked in a dry, intensely flavoured wok preparation using butter, dried chilli, garlic, ginger, shallots, curry leaves, oyster sauce, and soy sauce. Unlike wet crab dishes such as chilli crab or black pepper crab, the dry-style preparation results in a coating of aromatic, caramelised seasoning that clings to the shell and meat. The texture of the crab meat is firm and slightly sweet, with a savoury, mildly spicy, and umami-rich flavour profile. The dish is typically shared among two to three diners as part of a multi-dish Zi Char meal. Nutritionally, crab is a high-protein, moderate-fat seafood with notable levels of vitamin B12, zinc, selenium, and phosphorus. The dry-style preparation adds sodium and saturated fat from the sauce and butter. The glycemic index is low (estimated ~30) due to the minimal carbohydrate content, making it suitable for glycemic management when portion-controlled. Sodium content is elevated and warrants caution for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is halal-compatible when prepared without pork-derived ingredients. It is not suitable for those with crustacean shellfish allergies or soy allergies.

confidence_reason: No exact match for Asahi Dry Crab (Zi Char Style) exists in any approved primary nutrient database. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy cooked crab data with upward adjustments for sodium and fat to reflect dry-style Zi Char seasoning, cross-referenced against IMR Malaysia MyFCD stir-fried crab dish references. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database seafood classifications. Confidence is medium due to the adapted rather than exact source match.
notes: 1. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not consistently reported for crab in the USDA SR Legacy or IMR Malaysia MyFCD sources used for this record. 2. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the primary sources used. 3. All other -1 values reflect nutrients not reported in the adapted source combination. 4. The food name 'Asahidry-Japan' from the input record appears to be a field survey menu listing name combining a brand or style descriptor ('Asahi Dry') with a cuisine origin indicator ('Japan' likely referring to a Japanese-influenced dry preparation style or a restaurant branding choice). The canonical food name has been normalised to 'Asahi Dry Crab (Zi Char Style)' to reflect the actual dish category. 5. Served exclusively at Marina Seafood House - Boat Quay per input data; price SGD 12.00. 6. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (30) × available carbohydrate per serving (12.6 g for 300 g serving) / 100 = 3.8. 7. Halal status assessed as likely true for this dish category but cannot be confirmed without restaurant-specific certification; flagged as true based on ingredient profile (no pork, no alcohol in standard preparation).

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Drafted full record from input seed. Identified that input food name 'Asahidry-Japan' required normalisation to canonical dish name. Applied mandatory Singaporean multilingual naming (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi). Adapted USDA SR Legacy crab nutrients for Zi Char dry-style preparation. Estimated GI from University of Sydney GI Database analogous seafood data. Populated all three health context conditions including Type 2 Diabetes. Documented all -1 sentinel values (vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg) in notes.
